name: Pickled Mustard Greens

ingredients.item: fresh mustard greens (cải bẹ or gai choy), wilted and stems removed

method: In a large saucepan, combine all ingredients except the rice water, and bring to a boil over high heat. Once salt and sugar are dissolved, remove from heat and let cool.

Stir in the rice water and then pour into jars with the vegetables you want to pickle. Trim any bruised, yellow, or blemished sections off the mustard greens. Cut into 2-inch (5 cm) lengths and rinse well.
Trim any bruised, yellow, or blemished sections off the mustard greens. Cut into 2-inch (5 cm) lengths and rinse well.
Fill a medium saucepan halfway with water, bring to a boil, and then remove from heat.

When the water is no longer bubbling, stir in the mustard greens, making sure they are fully submerged. (You can place a small plate directly on top of them to weigh them down, if necessary. Just make sure it doesn’t fully cover the pot so that steam can escape.) Let sit for at least 4 hours or overnight. After the water cools, you can cover the pan to prevent contamination.

After soaking, the mustard greens should be light yellow and have a strong mustard smell. Drain and squeeze them dry. Empty the water from the saucepan, and then place the mustard greens back in it. Add the shallots and scallions, and toss well.

Pack the mustard greens mixture and chili peppers into two 47-ounce (1.4 L) sterilized glass jars (here), and then pour in enough brine to completely cover them. Use two bamboo skewers to press the vegetables into the brine. Close the lid tightly and leave the jar undisturbed on the kitchen counter for 3 days.
After the allotted time, taste a mustard green; if sour, they are ready to serve, but if not, continue to ferment for up to 2 more days.

To stop the fermentation process, simply drain all the pickling liquid. You can then store your pickled mustard greens in an airtight container or a fresh glass jar in the refrigerator for up to 1 month.
